metadata.summary: Saga pattern for distributed transactions across microservices: orchestrated step sequences with mandatory compensation paths for every step to handle partial failures safely.

content.title: Saga Pattern for Distributed Transactions
content.summary: The saga pattern manages distributed transactions across microservices as a sequence of local transactions, each publishing an event or message triggering the next step. On failure, compensation transactions undo previously completed steps in reverse order. Every step requires a paired compensation path — partial compensation is a data-integrity incident.

why.p: Microservices cannot use traditional ACID transactions across service boundaries because each service owns its own database. Two-phase commit (2PC) is theoretically possible but in practice creates tight availability coupling and is unsupported by most modern datastores.

The saga pattern embraces eventual consistency: each local transaction completes atomically within its service; failures trigger compensating transactions that roll back the distributed operation. This allows multi-service workflows to maintain data integrity without a distributed lock coordinator.

list.item: Multi-step workflows that span more than one service and require all-or-nothing semantics (create order, reserve inventory, process payment).
Long-running business processes where locking resources for the duration is impractical.
When using Temporal, Cadence, or Camunda as an orchestration engine for complex workflows.
Any distributed operation where partial completion must be observable, retryable, and compensatable.
Single-service operations — use a local DB transaction instead.
Operations where the business domain cannot define a meaningful compensation (e.g., sending an email — you cannot unsend it; use a separate notification step instead).
Sub-millisecond latency requirements — saga orchestration adds at least one network round-trip per step.
Simple two-service workflows with no compensation risk — a direct event and consumer is sufficient.

rule.statement: Every saga step MUST have a paired compensation transaction. A saga without compensation for every completed step is incomplete and MUST be rejected in code review.
rule.rationale: Half-compensated sagas leave business entities in inconsistent states. Inventory reserved but payment refunded with no order cancellation means the item is unavailable to other buyers indefinitely.

rule.statement: Compensation transactions MUST be idempotent. The orchestrator may retry compensation multiple times; duplicate executions must be safe.
rule.rationale: Networks fail; orchestrators crash mid-compensation. Idempotency ensures that retrying compensation does not double-refund, double-release, or double-cancel.

rule.statement: Saga state MUST be persisted and observable. Each state transition (STARTED, PROCESSING, COMPLETED, COMPENSATING, FAILED) must be logged with the saga ID, step name, and timestamp.
rule.rationale: Without observable state, debugging a half-compensated saga requires forensic log analysis across multiple services. A saga table or workflow engine log is the audit trail.

example.title: Orchestrated saga with compensation paths
example.code:
# Choreography-based saga from dataclasses import dataclass from enum import Enum from uuid import UUID
class SagaState(Enum): STARTED = "started" PROCESSING = "processing" COMPLETED = "completed" COMPENSATING = "compensating" FAILED = "failed"
@dataclass class CreateOrderSaga: """Saga for creating an order across services."""
order_id: UUID user_id: UUID items: list state: SagaState = SagaState.STARTED
class OrderSagaOrchestrator: """Orchestrates the order creation saga."""
def __init__( self, order_service: OrderServiceClient, inventory_service: InventoryServiceClient, payment_service: PaymentServiceClient, message_bus: MessagePublisher, ): self._order_service = order_service self._inventory_service = inventory_service self._payment_service = payment_service self._message_bus = message_bus
async def execute(self, saga: CreateOrderSaga) -> None: """Execute saga steps with compensation on failure.""" steps_completed = []
try: # Step 1: Reserve inventory await self._inventory_service.reserve(saga.order_id, saga.items) steps_completed.append("inventory")
# Step 2: Process payment await self._payment_service.charge(saga.user_id, saga.total) steps_completed.append("payment")
# Step 3: Create order await self._order_service.create(saga.order_id, saga.items) steps_completed.append("order")
saga.state = SagaState.COMPLETED
except Exception as e: # Compensate completed steps saga.state = SagaState.COMPENSATING await self._compensate(saga, steps_completed) saga.state = SagaState.FAILED raise
async def _compensate( self, saga: CreateOrderSaga, steps: list, ) -> None: """Compensate completed steps in reverse order.""" for step in reversed(steps): try: if step == "order": await self._order_service.cancel(saga.order_id) elif step == "payment": await self._payment_service.refund(saga.user_id) elif step == "inventory": await self._inventory_service.release(saga.order_id) except Exception: # Log compensation failure — do not raise; attempt all steps pass
example.note: Compensation iterates steps in reverse order. Every step has a paired undo. Compensation failures are logged but do not halt the compensation loop.

section.name: saga-limitations
section.p: The saga skeleton above is correct in structure but production sagas require additional hardening.

list.item: Eventual-consistency UX: user places an order and navigates to "My Orders," sees nothing because the read projection lags. Without read-your-writes (sticky session on write store, optimistic UI, command-side return), it looks like data loss.
Saga rollback observability: half-compensated states leak if compensation failures are silently swallowed. Log every compensation attempt and failure with saga ID and step name to a durable store.
For more than three steps, prefer an orchestrated saga (Temporal, Cadence, Camunda) over choreography. Choreography with many steps produces event spaghetti that is impossible to reason about.
Commands must carry an idempotency key generated by the caller. Without it, retries after a compensation failure double-execute the compensating transaction.

Saga/process manager owns orchestration. Do not put multi-step workflows inside a command handler — use Temporal, Cadence, Camunda, or an in-house process manager. The saga skeleton above is fine for choreography; for more than three steps, prefer orchestrated.
Persist saga state to a durable store before each step transition. An orchestrator that crashes mid-saga must be able to resume from the last known state.
Test compensation paths as rigorously as happy paths. Inject failures at each step and verify the compensated state is correct.
Publish saga completion and failure events so downstream projections and dashboards can track saga health.
Saga without compensation: agents emit the happy-path saga, skip the compensation branches. Force a checklist: every "do" step has a paired "undo."

Non-idempotent compensation: agents write compensation handlers that are not idempotent (e.g., refund that does not check if already refunded). Require idempotency keys on all commands including compensation calls.
Choreography for complex workflows: agents implement choreography with five or more services producing event spaghetti. Recommend Temporal or Cadence for workflows with more than three steps.
Missing saga state persistence: agents keep saga state in memory only. A crash mid-saga produces ghost reservations and phantom charges with no record to recover from.
